speaker: interviewer
text: The 29th of September, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stephen, would you like to introduce all these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sitting behind me is Danielle, my wife. This is Tanya, my middle daughter, Heidi, my eldest, and Trent, my step-son.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Claudia, who is not here today because she's working in Canberra, at the university there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you like to say about all of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm very proud of all of them, I must say. It's-- as you heard, there's been a long struggle to come to this stage in life but, like I said before, I'm a very happily contented man that I've managed to achieve what I have and to have such wonderful children and a wonderful wife with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Danielle, what do you think about Stephen's experiences' as a child impact on his life as an adult?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, think it probably isn't quite as obvious today as it probably was some years back. Would you agree?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not as outwardly obvious, but I think that-- I mean, recently, we had a wedding and I spoke about Steve. I had to speak about Stephen. I tried to focus on his early childhood and maybe what impact it actually had and something that I discovered about him, that I hadn't known-- and I've known him for 13 years-- was that he actually never had any formal education until he was nearly 10. And in a way, I think, when he came to Australia, finally, I think that really pushed him along to actually achieve what he has achieved. I think it's been-- probably that's been a positive aspect of it, that it actually pushed him to really succeed, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] particularly through study, because he really loved to study. But perhaps on the negative side, he-- I know, from what he's told me, that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he's felt a lot of insecurities because of the isolation and the fear that exists in a small  child hiding from-- for many years at such a young age. Hiding from possible danger and, you know, the possibility of extermination. Even if you don't totally understand, I think it leaves its impact. But I think that perhaps the greatest impact on his life is really that, basically, as is the case with many people who went through this, he's a little orphan, and he basically depends totally on his immediate family because the rest of the family, obviously, didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he had quite a-- even though he was an only child because he was only three when the war broke out and I would imagine that would've affected the size of the family-- Steve had quite a-- quite a substantial family back in Poland. Didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I did feel the fact that if the war hadn't broke out-- broken out-- broken out, I would have had siblings. I would have had brothers and sisters, and I've always felt this. I felt acutely about not having any-- any brothers or sisters, and I do tend to blame that on what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think this reflects in his need for and his feelings towards his family. I mean, if-- if his children ever fought, you know, it would-- he could never understand it, because normal sibling rivalry, to him, was something that was totally unexplainable considering that he never had it and he really would have wished to have had some.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think that it's made him very, very-- very family conscious and very, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a way, determined that his unit would be very strong. And he's always striven for that. And it is. It's a very strong family unit, in spite of the fact that we have an extended family through a re-marriage. You know? We're still, in a way, a very strong family, and I think he has a lot to do with-- with that fact. Tanya and Heidi and Trent, what is significant about having had a father and a  step-father who's lived through the Holocaust as a child? What have you noticed or felt or observed growing up with your dad and step-father? Tanya?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I actually did a [INAUDIBLE] assignment at school on my dad, and so that-- like, I learned all about what happened him-- to Dad through that, which was great to find out. But I mean, it's not discussed that much and, I mean, it-- yeah, it doesn't really-- I mean, it does affect me to know that my dad went through that, and my grandmother. Just can't comprehend it. Like, it's just pretty huge, and-- but yeah, it's made, as Danielle was saying, the family really important. Yeah, and we love our dad very much. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, as Tanya said, nothing's-- nothing's really been discussed, I guess. So what's happening now will probably be of great interest to all of us, because I don't-- Tanya's heard Dad's story, but it's not something that is brought up over dinner like it is with my grandmother who sort of speaks about it more outwardly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, we actually-- I mean, we've known, obviously, that Dad was young and, you know, lived in a ghetto, et cetera, et cetera. And we've more heard of Dad's life, I guess, sort of traveling, you know, after leaving Poland and then until he got here. So we-- I mean, we really don't know that much so, I mean, I'm really looking forward to hearing his story.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's something that is very hard to ask of your father, because you don't want to, you know, put him through that pain of asking him what his story was. But I have to agree with everyone here, it's just the family that's-- you know, his whole orientation is towards his family because he just adores all of us so much, but not as much as we  adore him. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. That's OK. Is he special to you, too?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's great. That's great. Danielle, is there anything else that you'd like to say about Stephen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Apart from the fact that he's a very special person? [LAUGHTER] Well, one thing that Steve did do for me, personally, was that he actually [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] reconnected me with my Jewish heritage, because I didn't go through the Holocaust but I was born in France, and my father was taken and died a few months before I was born. And the reaction of my mother was actually to try to get me to forget that I was Jewish. It was a normal reaction with many people who were petrified that it would happen again. And so consequently, I didn't really want to be part of the Jewish community. And in fact, the first time I married, I married a non-Jewish-- Trent's father-- lovely person, non-Jewish person. And when I first met Steve, I actually was quite petrified about reentering this danger zone. But somehow, there's something so special about him that, gradually and slowly, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he sort of managed to reconnect me to my heritage. Now, I think that it takes a very special man to be able to do that, because I certainly was looking to move away from the Jewish society rather than to come-- to come back to it. So-- and now I'm really happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took her to a lot of bar mitzvahs when we first met. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I used to hide in the [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: All the children-- all my friend's children were going through bar mitzvah [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Stephen, is there anything else that you would like to say about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can say that I wish I hadn't experienced those things that I did. But then again, I may have been a different person. Who's to know? I'm glad it's just turned out this way. I would have perhaps-- not perhaps. I'm sure I would have liked us to have had a much bigger family, and I do miss the fact that I've never had any cousins and aunts and uncles and grandparents. And I'm sure every Jewish person who's lost that in the Holocaust feels the same. It's a very deep loss. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But otherwise, I think I'm happy at last.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you Stephen, Danielle, Tanya, Heidi, and Trent. Thank you for sharing your experiences with the project.

speaker: interviewee
text: This old photograph was the only one we managed to salvage. It's a-- taken approximately in 1920 and it shows-- the little boy is my father, and that's his parents and his sister. The parents and the sister perished during the war. And that's my father about the age of 10.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know the names of my grandparents, but I think his sister was Lola, I think. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Bochnia, the 16th of June, 1935, my parents' wedding photo. My father, Edward, and my mother, Janina.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a group photo from the same wedding. The only survivors on this photograph was that little boy right there in front, called Dolek, who's now living in Israel, and his father is at the back. There are a few-- my mother's sisters. They-- she had five sisters. They all perished. My mother-- my father's sister is right behind him, and his parents are just to the right of her. And all these people died during the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father and I in 1937, so I must've been about, what, nine months old? I had a very big head.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father looked a bit like Clark Gable, [LAUGHS] but they all looked like Clark Gable in those days.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the Polish way of spelling Romania and Bucureszte, which is the name of the capital city, Bucharest, on the 10th of November, 1944. That's myself and my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a group shot of the 1954 class of [INAUDIBLE] Boys High School. I'm sitting down in the front. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm the second one from the left. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] With my-- the teacher standing at the back is Mr. [? Kresna, ?] the English master.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Heidi, what-- what photograph is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was about 1977? [LAUGHS] It's so cute. I was probably about seven and Tanya probably five. Claudia would've been 18 months.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Heidi's wedding, which took place on the 3rd of September, 1995. That's me and Tanya next to me, and of course, Heidi. Mother, Paula, and my youngest daughter, Claudia, on the far right, or left, of the picture, whichever way you want to look at it.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in the back yard of the house here, where the wedding ceremony took place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this is a picture of my three daughters. Now, one that wasn't able to be with us here today is Claudia on the left of your screen. And we miss her a lot but she has to work in Canberra. So at least I hope that she will enjoy seeing herself, even if it's only in the photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Who are the other two?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the other two? Oh, that's-- sorry. [LAUGHTER] That's Heidi and Tanya.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding photo to Danielle, which was in 1980--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- it was seven years ago this December, so it was 1988. We were married in a synagogue. We were married, actually, twice. We were married first-- we eloped to Barbados and were married by a judge in-- on the island. And then, exactly to the hour and 12 months later, we married in the synagogue here in Sydney with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape five of the interview with Stephen Muller. It's the 29th of September, 1995. We are in Sydney, Australia, and that's also the end of the whole interview.
